---
title: "Statistical Analysis of Online Shopping Behavior and Purchase Conversion"
subtitle: "MAS291 Computer Project"
author: |
  Lương Triều Vỹ – QE200035  
  Phạm Bửu Thịnh – QE200023  
  Nguyễn Đức Duy – QE200054  
  Nguyễn Quốc Vương – QE200066  
  Đinh Đỗ Gia Huy – QE200022
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%d %B %Y')`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    number_sections: true
    theme: flatly
    css: styles.css
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE,
                      fig.align = "center", out.width = "90%")
dir.create("r-library", showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)
.libPaths(c(normalizePath("r-library"), .libPaths()))
if (!file.exists("results/task1_structure_quality.csv")) source("analysis.R")
read_result <- function(name) read.csv(file.path("results", name), check.names = FALSE)
required_report_packages <- c("DT", "plotly")
missing_report_packages <- required_report_packages[
  !vapply(required_report_packages, requireNamespace, logical(1), quietly = TRUE)
]
if (length(missing_report_packages) > 0) {
  stop("Missing report packages: ", paste(missing_report_packages, collapse = ", "),
       ". Run setup_packages.R, then Knit again.")
}
```

# Introduction

This project analyzes online shopping sessions from the UCI Online Shoppers Purchasing Intention Dataset. The population of interest is online shopping activity on an e-commerce platform. The observed dataset contains 12,330 independent sessions. Revenue divides the data into 1,908 purchase sessions and 10,422 non-purchase sessions.

The analysis studies engagement duration, conversion, differences between purchase outcomes, and the relationship between product-page count and total product-page duration.

## Full dataset evidence - 12,330 sessions

The table below contains the complete dataset used in the project. `Session_ID` is the sequential identifier created for each online shopping session. The final three binary columns (`Revenue_int`, `Weekend_int`, and `Returning_int`) were derived for statistical calculations. Use the search box, column filters and page controls to inspect all 12,330 observations without expanding the report vertically.

```{r dataset-evidence}
dataset_evidence <- read_result("dataset_full_12330.csv")
DT::datatable(
  dataset_evidence,
  rownames = FALSE,
  filter = "top",
  class = "stripe hover cell-border compact",
  options = list(
    pageLength = 25,
    lengthMenu = c(10, 25, 50, 100),
    scrollX = TRUE,
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    deferRender = TRUE,
    search = list(caseInsensitive = TRUE)
  )
)
```

# Task 1 - Exploratory Data Analysis

## Structure and data quality

```{r}
knitr::kable(read_result("task1_structure_quality.csv"), align = c("l", "r", "l"),
             table.attr = 'class="report-table"')
```

There are no missing values. Exact duplicate feature vectors are reported but retained because sessions with identical behavior may still represent genuine observations. Both groups exceed the required minimum of 500 observations.

## Descriptive statistics

```{r}
task1_stats <- read_result("task1_descriptive_statistics.csv")
numeric_cols <- vapply(task1_stats, is.numeric, logical(1))
task1_stats[numeric_cols] <- lapply(task1_stats[numeric_cols], function(x) round(x, 3))
knitr::kable(task1_stats, table.attr = 'class="report-table wide-table"')
```

Purchase sessions view more product pages and spend more time on product-related pages on average. Non-purchase sessions have higher bounce and exit rates. Means exceed medians for several behavioral variables, while positive skewness and high-end IQR outliers show strong right-skewness.

## Visualizations

```{r, out.width='88%', fig.align='center'}
knitr::include_graphics(c("figures/task1_duration_histogram.png",
                          "figures/task1_duration_boxplot.png"))
```

The histogram uses 300-second intervals for all 12,330 sessions. Most sessions are concentrated in the lower-duration bins, and the frequencies decline as duration increases, showing a strongly right-skewed distribution. The last bar is an overflow bin for sessions above 6,000 seconds, so no observations are removed. The boxplot compares purchase and non-purchase sessions and displays the 0–6,000 second range for readability; more extreme observations remain included in all calculations and their IQR counts are reported in the descriptive-statistics table.

```{r, out.width='88%', fig.align='center'}
knitr::include_graphics(c("figures/task1_conversion_by_month.png",
                          "figures/task1_conversion_by_visitor.png"))
```

Each monthly bar is calculated independently as purchase sessions divided by all sessions observed in that month, so the bars are not components that should sum to 100%. Purchase outcome also varies across visitor types. These descriptive differences are associations and should not be interpreted as causal effects.

# Task 2 - Inference for One Population

## Population mean

We test whether mean product-page duration differs from 1,200 seconds using a two-sided one-sample t procedure at the 5% significance level.

```{r}
knitr::kable(read_result("task2_one_mean.csv"), table.attr = 'class="report-table"')
```

The estimated mean is approximately 1,194.7 seconds. The p-value is greater than 0.05, so there is insufficient evidence that the population mean differs from 1,200 seconds.

## Population proportion

We test whether the conversion proportion differs from 15% using a large-sample two-sided z procedure.

```{r}
knitr::kable(read_result("task2_one_proportion.csv"), table.attr = 'class="report-table"')
```

The estimated conversion rate is approximately 15.47%. The p-value is greater than 0.05, so there is insufficient evidence that conversion differs from the selected 15% benchmark.

# Task 3 - Comparison of Two Populations

## Difference between two means

Purchase and non-purchase sessions are compared using Welch's independent-samples t procedure because group sizes and variances differ.

```{r}
knitr::kable(read_result("task3_two_means.csv"), table.attr = 'class="report-table"')
```

Purchase sessions spend approximately 806 additional seconds on product-related pages. The difference is statistically significant and practically substantial.

## Difference between two proportions

The returning-visitor proportions of purchase and non-purchase sessions are compared using a pooled z test and an unpooled confidence interval.

```{r}
knitr::kable(read_result("task3_two_proportions.csv"), table.attr = 'class="report-table"')
```

The returning-visitor shares differ significantly. This comparison concerns group composition and does not by itself prove that new visitors have a higher individual probability of purchase.

# Task 4 - Simple Linear Regression

X is the number of product-related pages viewed, and Y is total product-page duration in seconds. These variables form an interpretable engagement model: viewing more product pages should generally require more total browsing time. To keep interaction reliable on presentation computers, the chart displays a fixed stratified sample of 2,000 sessions and zooms to 0--200 pages and 0--12,000 seconds, where the main data cluster can be inspected clearly. The fitted line and every reported statistic still use all 12,330 observations.

```{r task4-interactive-chart, fig.height=7, out.width='100%'}
task4_data <- read_result("dataset_full_12330.csv")
task4_data$Purchase_outcome <- ifelse(task4_data$Revenue_int == 1, "Purchase", "No purchase")
task4_data$hover_text <- sprintf(
  "Session: %s<br>Pages viewed: %s<br>Duration: %.2f seconds<br>Revenue: %s",
  task4_data$Session_ID,
  task4_data$ProductRelated,
  task4_data$ProductRelated_Duration,
  task4_data$Purchase_outcome
)

task4_model_for_chart <- lm(ProductRelated_Duration ~ ProductRelated, data = task4_data)
regression_line <- data.frame(
  ProductRelated = seq(0, 200, length.out = 250)
)
regression_line$ProductRelated_Duration <- predict(task4_model_for_chart, newdata = regression_line)

set.seed(291)
no_purchase_rows <- which(task4_data$Revenue_int == 0)
purchase_rows <- which(task4_data$Revenue_int == 1)
chart_rows <- c(
  sample(no_purchase_rows, 1600),
  sample(purchase_rows, 400)
)
chart_data <- task4_data[chart_rows, , drop = FALSE]
no_purchase <- chart_data$Revenue_int == 0
purchase <- chart_data$Revenue_int == 1

task4_plot <- plotly::plot_ly() |>
  plotly::add_markers(
    x = chart_data$ProductRelated[no_purchase],
    y = chart_data$ProductRelated_Duration[no_purchase],
    text = chart_data$hover_text[no_purchase],
    name = "No purchase",
    hoverinfo = "text",
    marker = list(size = 5, opacity = 0.35, color = "#4F81BD")
  ) |>
  plotly::add_markers(
    x = chart_data$ProductRelated[purchase],
    y = chart_data$ProductRelated_Duration[purchase],
    text = chart_data$hover_text[purchase],
    name = "Purchase",
    hoverinfo = "text",
    marker = list(size = 5, opacity = 0.45, color = "#C0504D")
  ) |>
  plotly::add_lines(
    x = regression_line$ProductRelated,
    y = regression_line$ProductRelated_Duration,
    name = "Fitted regression line",
    line = list(color = "#D62728", width = 3),
    hovertemplate = "Pages: %{x:.1f}<br>Fitted duration: %{y:.2f} seconds<extra></extra>"
  ) |>
  plotly::layout(
    title = "Product pages viewed vs product-page duration",
    xaxis = list(
      title = "Number of product-related pages viewed",
      range = c(0, 200),
      dtick = 25
    ),
    yaxis = list(
      title = "Product-related duration (seconds)",
      range = c(0, 12000),
      dtick = 2000
    ),
    legend = list(orientation = "h", x = 0, y = 1.10),
    hovermode = "closest",
    margin = list(t = 95, r = 30, b = 75, l = 90),
    height = 620
  ) |>
  plotly::config(
    displaylogo = FALSE,
    responsive = TRUE,
    modeBarButtonsToRemove = list("lasso2d"),
    toImageButtonOptions = list(filename = "task4_regression_chart")
  )

task4_plot
```

During the presentation, hover over a displayed point to inspect its session ID, page count, duration and purchase outcome. Use the toolbar to zoom, select an area, pan and reset the axes. Click a legend item to hide or show a purchase group. The 2,000 displayed sessions consist of 1,600 non-purchase and 400 purchase sessions selected with a fixed seed for reproducibility. The static PNG in `figures/` shows all observations and remains available as a submission and offline fallback.

The scatter plot shows a strong positive and approximately linear relationship, with increasing spread at high page counts. The correlation is approximately $r=0.861$, indicating a strong positive linear association. The coefficient of determination is $R^2=0.741$, so the page count explains about 74.1% of the observed variation in total product-page duration; the remaining variation is associated with other factors and random error.

```{r}
knitr::kable(read_result("task4_regression_model.csv"), table.attr = 'class="report-table"')
```

The fitted equation is

$$\widehat{Y}=19.301+37.044X.$$

The intercept means that the fitted duration is about 19.3 seconds when zero product pages are recorded; it mainly anchors the line and has limited behavioral importance. The slope means that each additional product page viewed is associated with an average increase of about 37.04 seconds in total product-page duration.

## Test of the regression slope

At $\alpha=0.05$, the hypotheses are:

$$H_0:\beta_1=0 \qquad\text{versus}\qquad H_1:\beta_1\ne0.$$

The test statistic is $t=187.900$ with $12,328$ residual degrees of freedom. The two-sided p-value is below 0.001 and $|t|$ exceeds the critical value of approximately 1.960. Therefore, reject $H_0$: there is statistically significant evidence of a linear relationship between page count and total product-page duration. The 95% confidence interval for the population slope is approximately $(36.657,37.430)$ seconds per page, which also excludes zero. The residual standard error is approximately 973.58 seconds, showing that individual sessions can still deviate substantially from the fitted line. This model establishes association, not causation.

## Predictions

```{r}
knitr::kable(read_result("task4_predictions.csv"), digits = 2,
             table.attr = 'class="report-table"')
```

These are fitted **mean durations** and 95% confidence intervals for the mean response, not guaranteed durations for individual sessions. For example, at 50 product pages, the model predicts a mean duration of about 1,871.48 seconds (31.19 minutes), with a 95% confidence interval of approximately 1,852.90 to 1,890.06 seconds. A prediction interval for one future session would be wider.

# Task 5 - Final Interpretation

## Key findings

```{r}
knitr::kable(read_result("task5_key_findings.csv"), table.attr = 'class="report-table"')
```

The duration difference is both statistically significant and practically large: converted sessions spend roughly 13.4 additional minutes on product pages. The visitor-composition difference is statistically significant but requires cautious interpretation. The regression relationship is practically strong because product-page count explains a large proportion of engagement-time variation. In contrast, the overall mean and conversion rate are close to their selected benchmarks and are not statistically significant.

## Conclusions and implications

```{r}
knitr::kable(read_result("task5_implications.csv"), table.attr = 'class="report-table"')
```

## Limitations

The dataset covers anonymized sessions and does not include transaction amount, demographic information, or a repeat-user identifier. Several variables are strongly skewed, and the two purchase groups are unbalanced. The analyses rely on independence and large-sample assumptions. Observational associations do not establish causality; controlled experiments such as A/B tests are required for causal platform decisions.

# Data Source

UCI Machine Learning Repository, Online Shoppers Purchasing Intention Dataset:  
https://archive.ics.uci.edu/dataset/468/online+shoppers+purchasing+intention+dataset
